search_and_rescue_questions.text: The session described three buckets of MQL types in priority order. What are they, from highest to lowest priority?
options: Executive campaign leads, product signals, hand raisers
Hand raisers, product signals, other MQLs (events and marketing content)
Product signals, hand raisers, inbound content leads
Other MQLs, product signals, hand raisers

correct_feedback.label: Forest Preserver! Correct:
correct_feedback.explanation: The presenter explained the three MQL buckets in priority order: hand raisers are first and highest priority (someone requesting contact directly), product signals are second (paying or non-paying Amplitude users with expiring contracts, overages, or interest in new products), and other MQLs are third (people engaging with marketing content and events at a lower level of intent). SLA length decreases as priority increases — hand raisers have the shortest SLA.

incorrect_feedback.label: Fire Starter! Incorrect:
incorrect_feedback.explanation: The presenter explained the three MQL buckets in priority order: hand raisers are first and highest priority (someone requesting contact directly), product signals are second (paying or non-paying Amplitude users with expiring contracts, overages, or interest in new products), and other MQLs are third (people engaging with marketing content and events at a lower level of intent). SLA length decreases as priority increases — hand raisers have the shortest SLA.

search_and_rescue_questions.text: According to the session, what is the correct action in Salesforce if you receive an MQL that you believe was incorrectly routed to you?
options: Post in the team Slack channel asking who owns the correct territory
Escalate to your manager and ask them to handle the rerouting
Handle the reroute directly on the MQL page in Salesforce using the accept, disqualify, or reroute buttons — no manager or team channel needed
Disqualify the MQL and add a note explaining the routing error so the ops team can reassign it

correct_feedback.label: Forest Preserver! Correct:
correct_feedback.explanation: The presenter was emphatic: do not go to the team Slack channel and do not go to your manager for rerouting. The new process puts the accountability on the rep — you handle rerouting directly on the MQL page in Salesforce using the accept, disqualify, or reroute buttons. It is a one-stop shop.

incorrect_feedback.label: Fire Starter! Incorrect:
incorrect_feedback.explanation: The presenter was emphatic: do not go to the team Slack channel and do not go to your manager for rerouting. search_and_rescue_questions.text: The session explained what happens when an MQL goes inactive after a future event is logged. What causes it to drop out of your funnel?
options: If you log a future event more than 90 days out it automatically drops at the 90-day mark
If the future event date passes and you take no action on it, it drops out of your funnel due to inactivity and resurfaces to someone else
If you don't log at least two touches within 7 days of logging the future event, it drops out automatically
If the contact opens your email but doesn't respond within 14 days of the future event date, it is automatically disqualified

correct_feedback.label: Forest Preserver! Correct:
correct_feedback.explanation: The presenter explained: logging a future event protects an MQL from going into breach. But if the future event date arrives and passes with no action taken, the MQL drops out of your funnel due to inactivity and resurfaces to someone else. The key is that you cannot log a future event and walk away — you have to maintain activity on it.

incorrect_feedback.label: Fire Starter! Incorrect:
incorrect_feedback.explanation: The presenter explained: logging a future event protects an MQL from going into breach. But if the future event date arrives and passes with no action taken, the MQL drops out of your funnel due to inactivity and resurfaces to someone else. The key is that you cannot log a future event and walk away — you have to maintain activity on it.

search_and_rescue_questions.text: When disqualifying an MQL in Salesforce, the session emphasized that selecting the correct disqualification reason is important. Why?
options: It determines whether the MQL gets reassigned to another rep or returned to the general lead pool for redistribution

Your disqualification reason feeds directly back to the marketing team, who use it to decide whether to nurture, re-enrich, or drop the lead — wrong reasons create downstream problems for the whole team
It affects your personal SLA adherence score and is tracked on the Sales Performance Dashboard under disqualification accuracy
It triggers an automatic notification to your FLM who reviews all disqualifications in your weekly one-on-one

correct_feedback.label: Forest Preserver! Correct:
correct_feedback.explanation: The presenter was explicit about this: when you disqualify an MQL, you are giving direct feedback to the marketing team. They go and assess that disqualification, then either nurture it or re-enrich it and bring it back. If you select the wrong reason — for example, marking something as a competitor when it actually had bad data — marketing wastes time assessing it incorrectly and the lead comes back wrong. Picking the right reason matters for the whole funnel, not just your own queue.

incorrect_feedback.label: Fire Starter! Incorrect:
incorrect_feedback.explanation: The presenter was explicit about this: when you disqualify an MQL, you are giving direct feedback to the marketing team. They go and assess that disqualification, then either nurture it or re-enrich it and bring it back. If you select the wrong reason — for example, marking something as a competitor when it actually had bad data — marketing wastes time assessing it incorrectly and the lead comes back wrong. Picking the right reason matters for the whole funnel, not just your own queue.

search_and_rescue_questions.text: When going out of office, the session described a new Salesforce out-of-office process. What are the two key things a rep needs to do?

Fill out the out-of-office form in their Salesforce profile AND designate an alternate user to work their MQLs in their absence

correct_feedback.label: Forest Preserver! Correct:

He compared it to blocking your calendar, setting an away message in Gmail, and updating your Slack status — just one more step in the same routine.

search_and_rescue_questions.text: JT recommended a strategy for managing your 1,000 monthly credits. What did he suggest?
options: Spend all 1,000 in the first week to fill your pipeline early, then focus on working those contacts for the rest of the month
Split evenly — 500 in the first half of the month and 500 in the second half to maintain consistent prospecting throughout

Allocate around 700-800 credits in the first two weeks to get people in early and into sequences, then hold a bank of 200-300 credits for the final weeks to handle new contacts and close out the month strong
Hold all credits until the last week of the month when you have the clearest picture of pipeline gaps and can target precisely

Then hold a bank of 200-300 credits for the final two weeks to handle new contacts, deal needs, or end-of-month pipeline building. The logic: you want to work contacts during the month, not add them at the end when there is no time left to prospect.

The MEDDIC qualification for S0 and S1 is handled by the partner team. search_and_rescue_questions.text: Courtney explained why it matters to the PSM that partner-sourced opportunities are correctly tagged in Salesforce. What are the two reasons?

It ensures the partner gets paid when the deal closes — and it counts toward the PSM's S2 sourced opportunity goal, which is one of their core metrics
It triggers the operations team to review the deal for MEDDIC compliance and ensures the partner receives a formal co-sell agreement before the deal progresses past S3
It prevents duplicate opportunities from being created if the partner also logs the deal in their own CRM, and it flags the deal for executive sponsor assignment

correct_feedback.label: Forest Preserver! Correct:
correct_feedback.explanation: Courtney explained two clear reasons: first, the partner needs to be tagged so they get paid when the deal closes — if the opportunity closes without the partner tagged, the partner will ask why they did not receive their cut. Second, it counts toward the PSM's S2 sourced opportunity goal, which is one of their core metrics for the year. Without the tag, the PSM cannot count that deal toward their target. She noted that the partner tagging is handled by operations as part of a checks and balances process — AEs do not add the partner themselves — but they should make sure the PSM knows so the right people can ensure it is done correctly.

search_and_rescue_questions.text: Corey explained the concept of multiple pathways to the number in forecast methodology. What does it mean and why does it matter?
options: It means having backup deals ready to swap in if a primary deal slips — Amplitude requires AEs to maintain a shadow pipeline specifically for this purpose

It means structuring your commit call so that different combinations of deals can get you to the same number — so if you miss one deal you are not completely off the call because other deals or conversion scenarios can still get you there
It means calling both a best case and worst case number alongside your commit so leadership has a full range of possible outcomes to report to the street
It means diversifying your pipeline across multiple segments and verticals so that a downturn in one industry does not single-handedly destroy your quarter

correct_feedback.label: Forest Preserver! Correct:
correct_feedback.explanation: Corey explained that forecasting is a bit of science and a bit of art — the goal is multiple pathways to the number. Rather than calling specific deals in or out, you structure the call so different scenarios of conversion across your pipeline can all get you to the same number. He used an example: if you have four strong upside deals and you need three of them, you do not need to know which three — just that getting three of four gets you there. If one slips, you have other buffer from upside or create-and-close to make up the difference. He noted this is not always possible with a single large deal, but with multiple deals it is the right approach.

Next steps, close date, stage, forecast category, and new ARR — these must be updated immediately if anything changes, not held until the following Monday
Outreach sequence status, last contact date, executive sponsor, and AE rollup — these are the fields that drive the automated forecast submission in Outreach
Renewal commit, churn deal rollup, pipeline coverage ratio, and Gainsight health score — these are the renewal-specific fields that Corey monitors weekly

He said if you get an update on a call on a Wednesday that moves a deal forward or backward, those fields should be updated immediately — not held until Monday. He also noted that next steps being stale is always called out, and if a VP is messaging you on a Thursday about a stale next step it is because Nate flagged it during their forecast call.

search_and_rescue_questions.text: Corey described the difference between the new business forecast and the renewal forecast at a fundamental level. What is it?
options: New business tracks net new logos only — renewals track both upsells and downsells on existing customers regardless of whether it is a new product or an expansion
New business forecasts how much net new ARR you will close from land and expand deals — the renewal forecast is essentially the inverse, tracking how much ARR you will lose from churn
New business is submitted weekly and covers the current quarter only — renewals are submitted monthly and cover both current and next quarter simultaneously
New business has three forecast calls (worst case, commit, best case) — renewals use a single effective renewal rate percentage rather than dollar amount calls

correct_feedback.label: Forest Preserver! Correct:
correct_feedback.explanation: Corey explained clearly: the new business forecast answers how much net new ARR you will close this quarter from land and expand deals. The renewal forecast is essentially the inverse — it tracks how much ARR you will lose from churn. The renewal commit call is how much of your renewable base you expect to renew, and the churn commit call is the difference between the renewable base and the renewal commit. He also noted that renewals only require a commit call — unlike new business which has worst case, commit, and best case.

incorrect_feedback.explanation: Corey explained clearly: the new business forecast answers how much net new ARR you will close this quarter from land and expand deals. search_and_rescue_questions.text: Corey described a key mindset for building a defensible forecast. What question did he suggest asking about deals — and why is it the right frame?
options: Ask yourself: what is this deal worth to Amplitude? Framing the deal in terms of strategic value rather than ARR helps prioritize which deals deserve the most attention in a forecast

Ask yourself: not why is this going to close, but why would this not close? This flips the perspective to focus on risk and mitigation rather than optimism — which is exactly how leadership reviews forecasts at the VP and Nate level
Ask yourself: what would the customer say if I called them right now? Making the forecast customer-centric ensures it reflects actual deal momentum rather than internal assumptions
Ask yourself: what is the minimum I need from this deal to hit my number? Building your forecast around minimum viable outcomes reduces overcommitment and improves accuracy

correct_feedback.label: Forest Preserver! Correct:
correct_feedback.explanation: JT introduced this mindset from a previous forecasting session: ask not why is this going to close, but why would this not close. Corey validated it strongly: at the VP and Nate level, forecast reviews are focused on where the risk is — what is the thing that might happen that we are not anticipating. He said the most common mistake in forecast calls is presenting everything as positive reasons why deals will close, when the productive frame is how do we mitigate the negative to make sure the worst case does not happen. This is how forecasts are stress-tested up the chain.

search_and_rescue_questions.text: Corey explained how new ARR flows from Salesforce into the Outreach forecast. What is the correct understanding of beginning ARR, ending ARR, and new ARR?
options: Beginning ARR is the customer's contracted value at deal creation, ending ARR is the projected value after the deal closes, and new ARR is the difference — ending minus beginning. The new ARR field is what flows into the new business forecast
Beginning ARR is the AE's quota for the quarter, ending ARR is the total pipeline value across all opportunities, and new ARR is the amount still needed to hit quota
Beginning ARR is the customer's current spend, ending ARR is the proposed renewal amount, and new ARR is only calculated for expand deals — land deals use a separate ARR field
Beginning and ending ARR are always the same on land deals — new ARR only appears on expand deals where the customer is adding products or seats

correct_feedback.label: Forest Preserver! Correct:
correct_feedback.explanation: Corey explained: the beginning ARR is the customer's current ARR at the start of the deal (zero for a net new land). The ending ARR is the projected ARR after the deal closes. New ARR is calculated automatically as ending minus beginning. He gave the example: if you enter an ending ARR of 90K on a land opportunity with a beginning of zero, new ARR calculates to 90K. That new ARR field is what flows into the new business forecast in aggregate across all deals in your pipeline. He also clarified that if you have a primary quote, it will update the ending ARR automatically.

Forecasting services from Stage 4 means that by the time a deal closes, resources are already pre-assigned and can be looped in immediately.

options: The emotional connection and the logical proof point — a story needs both to appeal to the whole brain

The hook and the end result — the hook must capture attention in the first 30 seconds or the prospect is already gone, and the end result must draw a hard line showing the art of the possible with a specific, real metric

The customer introduction and the quantified outcome — naming the customer and the exact number is what makes an impactful story memorable

correct_feedback.label: Forest Preserver! Correct:
correct_feedback.explanation: Ari described impact as having two bookends. The hook — you have 30 seconds to capture someone's attention. He illustrated the missing end result by comparing a vague Under Armour story (personal and relevant but no hard number) to the complete version: 'They launched coaching tips improving retention by 30% and drove 3x more engagement among paying customers — would you like me to connect you with them?' The second version has both bookends and creates a natural follow-up action.

AEs should build the demo themselves first and then ask the SE to review it before the customer call — this way the SE can focus on answering technical questions rather than presenting
AEs should submit a formal SE brief via the Salesforce SE request box at least one week before the call — walk-in requests are not accepted

The Solution Engineering channel for general product questions or anything you want to ask the full SE team, and the pillar channels (e.g. pillar-analytics, pillar-experiment) for specific topic deep dives — both are monitored by SEs and the product team
The #ask-se channel for pre-sales technical questions, and the #se-feedback channel for post-call debriefs — both are managed by the SE manager
There is only one SE Slack channel — all questions regardless of topic go there and the SE manager routes them to the right person

search_and_rescue_questions.text: Matt described his approach to a first enterprise demo. What is the structure — and why does he start with a cookie cutter demo before going tailored?
options: He always starts with a highly tailored demo built specifically for the prospect's industry — a generic demo signals low effort and creates a poor first impression

He starts with a standard platform walkthrough using his own data set to show as much of the platform as possible — so the customer can react and identify what they care about most. He then builds something more tailored based on their reactions in a follow-up session.
He starts with a competitor comparison before showing the Amplitude platform — establishing differentiation before the demo makes the product features land with more impact
He always starts with the session replay feature because it is the most visually engaging — then uses the customer's reaction to determine how deeply to go into analytics

correct_feedback.label: Forest Preserver! Correct:
correct_feedback.explanation: Matt described his approach for an enterprise first demo: keep it cookie cutter with his standard data set and loosely touch as much of the platform as possible. The reason: the customer often doesn't know exactly what they are looking for yet, and seeing the full range of possibilities allows them to self-identify what they want to see more of. After the first demo, they might say 'I want to see detailed conversion, cohorting, and your experimentation capabilities' — and at that point Matt goes away and builds something specifically tailored to those needs for the next session.

search_and_rescue_questions.text: Matt explained the difference between the AE demo org (GTM) and the SE demo org. What is the key distinction?
options: The GTM org contains real customer data from Amplitude's own product — SEs use it to show live customer examples. The SE org uses synthetic data that cannot be shared with prospects.

The SE demo org is deliberately messy with complex, multi-layered data that allows SEs to demo virtually any part of the platform and get meaningful results. The AE GTM org is cleaner and more polished but less flexible for deep exploratory demos.
The AE GTM org contains industry-specific datasets for each vertical — SEs use a single generic dataset regardless of customer industry
There is no difference — AEs and SEs use the same demo environment, just with different login credentials

correct_feedback.label: Forest Preserver! Correct:
correct_feedback.explanation: Matt explained that SEs have access to a different demo org than AEs. The SE org is intentionally messy — having complex, layered data means SEs can demo almost any part of the platform and still get useful results. The messiness comes from all the SEs using it constantly. The AE GTM org (which includes AmplyStore and AmplyShop datasets) is cleaner and more polished, but less flexible for deep exploration. The separation exists by design: the SE org is built for maximum demo flexibility, while the GTM org is built for AE-led demonstrations with a cleaner experience.

search_and_rescue_questions.text: Matt described the recommended path for AEs who want to develop their own demo skills. What are the steps?
options: Complete all Amplitude Academy courses first, then request a formal demo certification from the SE team before attempting any customer-facing demos

Work through the GuideFlow demo scenarios Matt provides, then watch a live demo or recording to see the click-to-story articulation, then book time with Matt for a reverse demo — where he demos to you, then you demo back to him, and you discuss what felt easy versus hard
Shadow three live customer demos as an observer before attempting your own, then submit a self-recorded demo to the SE team for feedback
Build a demo independently using the GTM org, then ask your manager to review it — the SE team only gets involved for enterprise-level deals above a certain ARR threshold

correct_feedback.label: Forest Preserver! Correct:
correct_feedback.explanation: Matt laid out a clear progression: first, work through the GuideFlow click-through scenarios he provides — these simulate the demo environment and the steps involved. Second, watch a live demo or recording to see how the story is articulated alongside the clicks, since GuideFlow is interactive but not narrated. Third, once you have done both, book time with Matt for a reverse demo session — he demos a bit, you demo back to him, and you discuss what felt easy and what felt hard. He committed to doing this with anyone who puts the time in his calendar, and said every SE on the team would be happy to spend time developing AEs this way.

clips.title: Day 15b: Leveraging Solution Engineers (Why Sell Services & Services Forecasting)

search_and_rescue_questions.text: Nicolette made an important point about Amplitude's services pricing compared to competitors. What was it — and what compensation benefit did she mention for AEs?
options: Amplitude's services are priced at a premium compared to competitors — but the higher price signals quality and makes the deal feel more comprehensive to the customer

Amplitude actually sells services at a loss — charging roughly 25-50% of what a competitor like Adobe charges — because they believe so strongly in services driving customer success. PSP also counts as a second product for the AE comp plan.
Amplitude's services are priced identically to market rates — the key benefit is that attaching them generates a co-sell credit that counts toward partner revenue targets
Amplitude bundles services for free on all deals above $500K — below that threshold, services are priced at market rate

correct_feedback.label: Forest Preserver! Correct:
correct_feedback.explanation: Nicolette made two notable points. First, Amplitude sells services at a loss — charging approximately 25-50% of what a competitor like Adobe charges for similar services. The reason: Amplitude believes so strongly in services driving customer success that they subsidize them. This means AEs should be fully convicted when positioning services — there is no reason to hesitate given how reasonable the pricing is. Second, she noted that PSP counts as a second product for the AE's comp plan — another financial incentive for making sure PSP is included on deals.

search_and_rescue_questions.text: Corey explained a benefit of services forecasting specifically for PS and partner capacity planning. What does it enable?
options: It allows PS leadership to set pricing in advance for upcoming deals — so quotes can be generated without waiting for scoping conversations

It gives PS teams and partners advance visibility into upcoming projects so they can start capacity planning, earmark resources, and be ready to hit the ground running the moment a deal closes — rather than figuring out staffing after the fact
It triggers automatic partner assignments in Salesforce — so when a deal reaches Stage 5, a partner is already provisioned and receives a notification
It generates automated implementation timelines in Salesforce — so the customer can see the projected go-live date before signing the contract

correct_feedback.label: Forest Preserver! Correct:
correct_feedback.explanation: Corey explained a key operational benefit: right now, resources cannot be assigned to projects until a deal is closed — meaning every time a deal closes, the team has to figure out staffing from scratch. With services forecasting, PS teams and delivery partners can see everything coming down the pipeline for the next several months and start capacity planning well in advance. If more headcount is needed, they know early enough to act. Partners especially need this visibility to earmark their implementation resources. This directly reduces the lag between deal close and implementation kickoff — improving the customer experience and time to value.

search_and_rescue_questions.text: Ganit described the key scoping questions AEs need answers to in order to determine the right services package. Which of the following is one of them?
options: What is the customer's preferred Slack communication style — synchronous or async — since this determines how the PS team will manage the implementation project

How many teams or products are being supported, whether they want hand-holding for all of them or just an MVP team, and whether they have technical and business leads — this informs the level and scope of implementation services needed
Whether the customer has already signed their NDA and MSA — services cannot be scoped until the legal framework is in place
Whether the customer has a dedicated budget for services separate from their platform spend — if not, services should be blended into the platform price without being surfaced

correct_feedback.label: Forest Preserver! Correct:
correct_feedback.explanation: Ganit walked through the scoping questions AEs need to have answers to: how many teams or products are being supported; whether the customer wants hand-holding for all of them or just an MVP team who can then train others; whether they have technical and business leads; what use cases they want to deploy; whether there are migration needs or urgent timelines; and whether they already have a governance practice or taxonomy. These questions help PS determine the right package and scope. By Stage 4, the AE should already have most of this information from discovery — it should not be a new conversation.

These questions help PS determine the right package and scope. By Stage 4, the AE should already have most of this information from discovery — it should not be a new conversation.

search_and_rescue_questions.text: Nicolette explained that PSP does not have to be surfaced as a visible line item to the customer. How can it be handled instead — and what does this mean for the order form?
options: PSP can be included verbally without any documentation — the customer simply agrees to the engagement without a written scope of work

PSP can be blended into the platform fee as a fully opaque line item — it will not appear as a separate line on the order form, though it will generate an exhibit with statement of work language. It still counts as ARR.
PSP can be deferred to the renewal — it is added at renewal time rather than on the initial contract so as not to complicate the initial close
PSP can only be waived for strategic accounts with executive approval — for all other accounts it must be a visible line item so the customer understands what they are buying

correct_feedback.label: Forest Preserver! Correct:
correct_feedback.explanation: Nicolette advised AEs not to feel like they need to point PSP out with a magnifying glass — there is flexibility. PSP can be blended into the platform fee and made fully opaque to the customer, meaning it does not appear as a separate line item on the order form. However, it will generate an exhibit in the order form with statement-of-work language, so there is documentation — it just is not surfaced as a distinct line item. It still counts as ARR and still counts as a second product for the AE comp plan. She encouraged new AEs to work with peers and FLMs to understand the trade-offs of different approaches.

search_and_rescue_questions.text: Corey and Ganit described the new Partner Services fields added to Salesforce. What are they for — and when would you use them?
options: They track which delivery partner is being subcontracted by Amplitude to deliver implementation services — used whenever a partner is delivering on Amplitude's behalf

They capture situations where services are being sold or delivered entirely by a third party outside of Amplitude's contract — not subcontracted through Amplitude. Used for edge cases to document that the customer has implementation support even when it is not on Amplitude's order form.
They are used to request partner involvement in a deal — filling them out triggers a notification to the PSM for the region to assign a delivery partner
They track partner referral credits for deals where a partner introduced the customer — used to ensure the partner receives their commission on services revenue

correct_feedback.label: Forest Preserver! Correct:
correct_feedback.explanation: Corey explained that the Partner Services on Partner Paper and Partner Provider fields are for a specific edge case: when services are being sold or delivered entirely by a third party — not subcontracted through Amplitude, not on Amplitude's order form, but rather a completely external arrangement. This is different from Amplitude's normal delivery partner model. The fields let Amplitude document that the customer has some level of implementation support even in these cases. Corey noted these fields will usually be marked 'No' — they are for the exception scenarios. Filling them out helps ensure PSP Override Reasoning is not used incorrectly when a partner is handling services independently.
